critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design that handles most of the obvious methodological failure modes. I'll work through the genuine issues I can identify.

**Issue 1: The 14-day activity prerequisite creates a biased analysis population that may define away the target problem**

The methodology requires learners to have at least 14 days of observed activity before each observation window cutoff. This is motivated by avoiding cold-start sparsity, which is a real concern. However, early dropout — the stated target — disproportionately occurs among learners who were never very active to begin with. Learners who drop out in days 1–13 are systematically excluded from both training and evaluation. The model is therefore trained and evaluated on a population that has already survived a self-selection filter: they were engaged enough to generate 14 days of activity. This is not just a generalization concern; it structurally excludes the modal early-dropout pathway. The reported recall figures will not estimate recall over the full learner population, only over this survivor subset. If interventions are operationally applied to all enrollees (the 10% FPR threshold implies whole-population ranking), the model's real-world precision and recall will differ systematically from the reported values because the deployment population includes the excluded learners who had no 14-day history. The mechanism is straightforward: the model is calibrated and evaluated against a distribution that is stratified by minimum engagement, but will be scored against a distribution that is not.

Evidence that would resolve this: Compare the distribution of observed dropout rates in the 14-day-filtered population vs. the full enrollment population. If early-course attrition is a large fraction of total dropout (which MOOC literature consistently finds it is, often 30–50% within the first two weeks), then the exclusion is large enough to make the reported recall figures misleading as an operational estimate.

**Issue 2: Recall at fixed FPR is not the operational metric being described**

The design states the primary metric is 'recall at a fixed false positive rate of 10%' and describes it as 'what fraction of true dropouts can the model identify among the top 10% of learners by predicted risk.' These are not the same metric. Recall at fixed FPR means: among all learners, flag enough to reach a 10% false positive rate, then measure recall. Recall among the top 10% by predicted risk means: take the top decile by score, measure what fraction of those who are true positives constitutes of all true positives (i.e., recall at a fixed threshold where the flagged set is exactly 10% of the population). These coincide only when the positive base rate is such that 10% FPR corresponds to flagging exactly 10% of the population, which is not generally true. If the base rate of dropout is, say, 40%, then a 10% FPR may correspond to flagging far more than 10% of learners, or far fewer, depending on calibration. The operational constraint is a staffing one (capacity to reach 10% of learners), so the correct metric is recall among the top-k flagged (fixed volume), not recall at fixed FPR. This is not a minor labeling issue — the metric that aligns with the business constraint and the metric labeled as primary are different, and the stated metric may be impossible to directly control at deployment time.

Evidence: Compute both metrics on the validation set and report whether they diverge. If the dropout base rate is far from 50%, the divergence will be material.

**Issue 3: The baseline comparison does not isolate what it claims to isolate**

The design states the comparison 'isolates the value of sequence and calendar information, not merely the choice of algorithm' by giving both models equivalent hyperparameter search budgets. However, the baseline model (logistic regression on aggregate features) and the primary model (GBM on the full feature set including sequence features) differ along two dimensions simultaneously: (a) the feature set, and (b) the model class. There is no GBM trained on the baseline feature set alone, and no logistic regression trained on the full feature set. Therefore the observed lift could be attributed to the model class difference rather than the feature difference. A sequence-informed logistic regression would separate algorithmic lift from feature-information lift. A GBM trained only on aggregate features would do the same. Neither is included. This is a missing ablation, not just an interpretation concern — the causal claim being made ('sequence and calendar features improve recall') requires at minimum one of these controls.

Evidence: Add a GBM trained on baseline features only. If that model performs comparably to the full GBM, the lift was from the algorithm, not the features.

**Issue 4: Cohort-based test split controls course memorization but not temporal distribution shift in learner behavior**

The split assigns distinct cohorts to train, validation, and test, which does control for course-specific timing pattern memorization. However, all cohorts are from the same platform during an 18–24 month window. Cohort identity and temporal position are confounded: cohort 5 is always the most recent. Any temporal drift in learner behavior (e.g., platform UX changes, changes in learner demographics as the platform scales, curriculum updates across cohorts) will appear as cohort-specific differences. The design acknowledges this partially by noting that platform changes after month 18 affect both models equally — but that handles only one direction of the confound. Drift in the learner population composition (younger learners, more international enrollment, etc.) is not controlled by the chronological split and will be absorbed into 'cohort 5 is different.' The per-course AUC diagnostic will detect course-level overfitting but will not distinguish between 'this cohort's courses are harder' and 'this cohort's learner population is different.' This means the validation of generalization is weaker than claimed.

Evidence: Report demographic composition by cohort. If cohort 5 differs substantially in age, prior education, or geography from cohorts 1–3, the test set is measuring both temporal generalization and demographic shift simultaneously, and the design cannot separate them.

**Summary of genuine issues:**

Issue 1 (14-day filter) is the most fundamental: it defines away a large fraction of the target population and makes the reported recall figures non-representative of the deployment setting. This is a structural flaw, not a risk.

Issue 2 (metric misdefinition) is real but potentially fixable with a clarification or metric swap; the direction of the error is predictable given the base rate.

Issue 3 (confounded baseline) is a genuine missing ablation that prevents the causal claim from being established, even if the performance lift is real.

Issue 4 (cohort/temporal confound) is a real limitation but acknowledged as partially out of scope; the design's claim about what it does not establish is honest.

Of these, Issue 1 is sufficient to undermine the validity of the experiment as a measurement of the stated operational goal.
all_issues_raised: The 14-day activity filter systematically excludes early dropouts — the modal target case — making reported recall figures non-representative of the deployment population where no such filter applies.

Recall at fixed FPR (10%) and recall among the top-10%-by-score are not the same metric; they coincide only under a specific base-rate assumption, and the operationally correct metric is the latter, not the former.

The baseline comparison confounds feature value with model class because there is no GBM trained on baseline features alone nor a logistic regression on the full feature set, leaving causal attribution of lift ambiguous.
Cohort identity is confounded with calendar time, so the test split cannot distinguish generalization across course content from generalization across learner population drift.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 14-day activity filter systematically excludes early dropouts — the modal target case — making reported recall figures non-representative of the deployment population where no such filter applies.

Recall at fixed FPR (10%) and recall among the top-10%-by-score are not the same metric; they coincide only under a specific base-rate assumption, and the operationally correct metric is the latter, not the former.

The baseline comparison confounds feature value with model class because there is no GBM trained on baseline features alone nor a logistic regression on the full feature set, leaving causal attribution of lift ambiguous.
Cohort identity is confounded with calendar time, so the test split cannot distinguish generalization across course content from generalization across learner population drift.
